sentences.text: Nope.
Well, people with whom I traveled from Poland to Germany.
We traveled three weeks.
It's a long journey, complicated journey.
And I befriended some of the people and maintained contact with them for a while.
And then life separated us.
In Rome, I lived in a-- what they refer to as a kibbutz.
This was a group of orthodox people who led a communal life.
There were about 100 of us, 120, living in the villa near Rome, in little Roma.
The goal of the kibbutz was to proceed to Palestine, but it was illegal.
So they were waiting for the legal immigration.
On the [INAUDIBLE]
Yes.

It's described in my book on-- this is the same ship which Leon Uris turned into a story, There was a hunger strike in the harbor of La Spezia, and after the hunger strike and all kinds of other events, we were eventually given certificates, which were issued by the British.
And we went to Palestine.
Well, I look at it from the distance of time and objectively.
I think the issue was very small.
There were about 7,000 Jews at that time.
Secondly, its resources were relatively limited, and they tried their utmost to receive us as well as they could.
It was 19-- May, to be precise, 1946.
I found my-- some members of my family in Tel Aviv.
I had many friends among those with whom I traveled, or lived in Italy.
And then I made new friends in Israel at the University where I studied, and then eventually in the Haganah.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, I had documentation that I am a graduate of a, not high school, but the equivalent of two years college.
So I qualified for admission as a matriculated student.
Secondly, a member of the Jewish Agency at that time, Emil Shmorak from Lvov, was a friend of my father.
And he was kind enough to take interest in me and introduced me to the registrar of the Hebrew University, Dr.
Posnaisky. ?] So I was admitted without any difficulty because of my documents.
But what's more important, I was given free tuition.
And Dr.
Shmorak arranged a small stipend for me of 20 Israeli-- or Palestinian pounds a month, which allowed me to subsist in Jerusalem and study.
No, not really.
You had to have very powerful connections for that.
Then I published my book, and I suddenly became a man of means, because I got 200 Palestinian pounds for my book.
So I was much more relaxed.
Difficult?
No.
First of all, I was there at a very interesting time.
And I was privileged to be still in the days when the old Zionist i-- idealism permeated the country.
I lived in a kibbutz for a while, and then at the Hebrew University.
I was very happy here and very proud to be part of it.
I was a member of the Haganah, and then I graduated from the Haganah commander school-- [INAUDIBLE] commander school, which was a privilege.

It was perhaps physically very hard, and maybe also emotionally hard, because I had to adapt to sabras and people with a different outlook from mine, who didn't have the horrible burden of the memories of the Holocaust.
But by and large, if I did not insist on bringing up the Holocaust or show a deep sense of sorrow for myself, I was accepted and functioned among them reasonably well and was quite happy.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] Then I was part of the creation of the state of Israel, so these were historical days.
It was a privilege.
Yes, but not on the level in which I was.
No.
No, no.
Well, I was in touch with my brother after the war all the time.
However, I left him in Poland with the understanding that I would get him out.
But I got trapped in difficulties of living as a displaced person in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first Germany, then Italy, then in the La Spezia affair.
And my conditions in Israel were such that it was not really possible to bring him over.
And besides, I-- I couldn't get a permit for him.
Then the war broke out.
So I met him again, 1951, in December, on my way to the United States.
In London.
He was sent with a group of children to London in 19-- I believe-- 45, sometime in the autumn, probably September, October.
Yes.
Well, I was here twice.
I was here once on a mission of the Israeli government to be sent to finish my graduate studies at Columbia.
And I was attached to the United Nation's Mission for a year and a half.
I married my wife here, and we went back to Israel.
I carried on my career until she became very ill.
Olga Gruss Lewin.
Eventually, she died, unfortunately, 1963.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But again, a variety of experiences [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] raised some questions in my mind.
And I just did not wish to lead people, and I didn't want to be a politician.
So I had no choice because my record is such that I couldn't just step aside.
And I didn't wish to continue, either government career or military career or a political career.
And I didn't want to go back to academia.
So I had no choice.
You went into--
Economics, to make a living.
And I started as a messenger boy on Wall Street.
Yes.
Well I, as you can see, have no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sensitivity on the subject, and therefore I communicated with a variety of people on what has happened.
And survivors, other survivors visited me.
So my children are fully aware of my background.
Besides, I became involved in the process of beatification of this Archbishop Metropolitan.
I was called to testify as a witness, because I'm one of the few people who lived with him and worked in his archives.
I was called to testify in the process of beatification in Rome.
What it involved is that my testimony was concentrated on two things.

One, on the behavior of this Archbishop Metropolitan during the Holocaust, the fact that he came out with an open, pastoral letter condemning the killing of Jews and warning his flock not to participate, directly or indirectly, in this horror.
And the pastoral letter's title is, 'You Shall Not Kill.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is one point.
Secondly, he also organized, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the monastic orders in his diocese, formal action of saving Jews.
And to my amazement, with time, it became apparent to me that there were more than 200 people saved.
It's recorded.
There's a file on the subject in the Yad Vashem.
Clement was recognized as Righteous by Yad Vashem.
So was Steck-- Father Steck and the Studites.
They have some questions about the Metropolitan because of controversies which were injected by Poles, by KGB, and so forth.
No, he wasn't beatified.
It's a lengthy process.
It's still ongoing, yes.
The formal, first phase of the process is finished.
And I helped with Postulation, as it is called, and collecting documents and doing research.

Because they quickly became aware that I can testify not only to the activities connected with saving and sheltering Jews, but I am also able to assist in unraveling the complex political and economical social situation in this part of the world during the lifetime of this individual.
Oh, yes.
Part of my life, because they see the picture of the Metropolitan and his brother is in my bedroom.
These are people, my benefactors, thanks to whom I live.
Father Marek, as long as he was alive, used to come and visit me and visit my children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many other monks came and went.

The older boys are named Andrew and Clement to commemorate the fact that these two extraordinary human beings saved not only my life, but befriended Jews at a time when nobody was interested to befriend Jews, and had compassion.
Very rare phenomenon in this part of the world at that time.
[LAUGH]
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have made clear that I am brought up as a Jew.
I am a Jew and I think like a Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whether this is a particular advantage, my answer is no.
Why and how, it's a long discussion, not related here.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always could dissolve in the Christian world at choice, because among Ukrainians, to this day, I'm not recognized as a non-Ukrainian.
The same applies to the Poles.
So the moment of truth came when my first born was born, and I asked myself whether I should let him be circumcised, which was a curse.
I have to remind you of my walk in Lemberg with the poor brother, [INAUDIBLE], was taken to a door of a building to have his natural documents checked.
But once I decided to circumcise my oldest son, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] losing a lot in expression, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- I cast my dice.
If I were confronted with the decision today, I'm not sure I would do that.
No--
No.
They know of-- about the Holocaust.
Involvement in Holocaust is, I think, a debatable activity, because one shouldn't dwell in this strategy.
One should remember.
One should respect it.
Well, my older boys are raised as Orthodox Jews.
They went to Sunday school and had their bar mitzvahs and-- organized by my non-Jewish wife, who's an expert on the subject.
And my older son goes to a synagogue and raises his children in a traditional Jewish way.
My youngest son is half way.
Neutral.
Well, they're too small.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How can one be aware of these experiences?
I don't think you can.
No, you cannot.
No, that is a wrong approach.
To me, we deal with a world which is surrealistic.
It's like a her-- Hieronymo-- Hieronymus Bosh picture.
A normal human being and mind cannot imagine that, and shouldn't.
Because it doesn't fall into categories of normal human behavior.
End of story.
Yes.
Well, other survivors knew the man.
And actually, one couple, my-- my friend's wife's-- wife's brother was shot by this man.
So she gave my name to the Department of Justice.
So they asked me, did you know the man?
I said, no, but I was there.
Do you know of him?
Yes.
Consequently, they came to a conclusion that they can utilize my testimony because I'm reasonably literate and--
The man was-- I forgot the name of the lawyer of the Department of Justice.
The offendant is Osidach, was name his name.
Osidach, O-S-I-D-A-C-H.
He was the commanding officer of the Ukrainian police in Rawa Ruska.
Personally, not.
But I knew of him because it was a small town.
And the Jewish community was afraid of him, and rightly so.
At the trial, I was asked to identify the name and his function in the town.
What was the Ukrainian police?
What was his role in the town?
And questions of a general nature, which I have answered.
And how did they get to Rawa Ruska and so forth.
Having answered these questions, it became [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clear that I don't know the man personally.
But I can say a great deal about the Ukrainian police, especially that I lived among the Ukrainians as a Ukrainian.
And I have a very intimate knowledge of these people.
Not in a way--
No.
Not in a way where I would assume that I'm being briefed.
They tried to determine who I am, what I know, and how to use me, but not what I should say or what the questions would be.
Yes.
In Philadelphia.
I don't know.
But I would assume, judging from other related experiences-- because I advised at that time a bank in Philadelphia, and a corporation-- probably in the early '80s. 1980, '81.
No,
No.
No, I was on the witness stand.
And just he was sitting there.
I didn't even--
It was a Ukrainian born in the United States, and he asked me silly question about the uniforms of the Ukrainian police, their weapons, what was their authority.
He obviously was not very familiar with the subject and the whole cross-examination, which was designed to point out, to destroy my credibility, was-- just back-fired.
That's all.
Not while I was there.
He has not reported that he was serving in the Ukrainian police, and that he was a commanding officer of the Ukrainian police in Rawa Ruska.
He has-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did not disclose it when-- in the usual questionnaire.
So he obtained American citizenship illegally.
And consequently, as a war criminal, he was stripped of it.
No.
There was, eventually, a verdict, after other testimonies were taken.
No, I wasn't interested.

And eventually the court decided that he has violated the laws of the United States and will be stripped, according to the-- to the legislation, which is-- was introduced by Elizabeth Holtzman, and some other legislation on the subject of his citizenship.
He appealed.
And the appeal, I think, went up to the Supreme Court and was rejected.
But he died before being deported.
There were some threats on my life, as well as threats on the judge's life.
I have no idea, and this was reported to me by the FBI.
No.
No.
Yes, but most of them are dead.
Most of them died over the years and today, I'm probably one or two survivors of the old group which existed when I was with them.
But I have very friendly relationship with the order, which still exists in Poland.
They know of me.
And my brother and I, within our means, make a contribution.
They are rebuilding their monasteries.
And as they say, that I'm a member of the community.
End of story.
Yes.
They come and go, yes.
I still am contacted by them, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's a strange relationship because they-- they recognize me as a member of the community, although I'm Jewish, although I'm married, I have nothing to do.
I'm part of their--
The old ones, obviously, Roman.
Never mind, after so many years, they still persist calling me that.
Actually, the-- one group of the monks, who was with me in the Carpathian mountains, later on found its way to Canada, to Woodstock, Ontario, and they lived there.
I visited them there.
No.
No.
I keep m-- very close contact with this church, with the bishops and so forth.
And they know of me, and occasionally, I'm participate-- I'm asking to participate in programs to commemorate the Metropolitan, yes.
Which I do with pleasure.
I am always happy to testify to the greatness of the man, his assistance, his humanity, and his values.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews in Poland considered him, if you know the expression, [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
That's right.
No.
No.
Very much so.
And he says he has two heritages.
And he has very close relationship with my brothers-- with his brothers.
Sorry.
Accident.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Second point, I was very close to Belzitz, so I saw the destruction of the community, or the process of destruction of community at a very early stage.
And I realized that we all carry a death sentence because of the place I was and knowledge of the topography.
Three, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was, probably since childhood, more aggressive and more enterprising-- aggressive not in a negative sense.
Three, and because of my father's contact with the Metropolitan, who was my benefactor, and his brother.
And finally, because of resiliency. to submerge in monastic life with an Eastern monastic community is not an easy job.
About what?
No, no, no, no.
Having fought in the Polish army and having spent three years in combat in Israel, I'm not pestered by these things at all.
When I see the [INAUDIBLE] was there, mitzvah tanks run aground, I find it very amusing, you see, and entertaining.
When I see women rabbis in the synagogue, which I occasionally might visit, I see trampling on tradition, which cause me a lot of harm, because I was chased in those days because I'm a Jew.
So seeing people trample-- trampling on their own traditions makes me angry.
But otherwise, I am a neutral observer, as I have won-- I have fought my battles.
I won some.
I lost some, as everybody else.
I don't find my story unique or special.
It's unique in only one point, that I was fortunate not to be in the clutches of the Germans for too long.
So I was not exposed to this horror of dehumanization, as my friend Father Petras who, because of his assistance to the Metropolitan and variety of activities which were anti-Nazi, ended in Dachau.
I didn't.
Oh, you speak about the book?
I got a phone call from the Shoah Institute, and a charming young lady, Victoria, who inquired whether I would like to testify.
My answer was yes, because I wish to take every opportunity to create a record and a memory of those people who perished.
[INAUDIBLE] sitting here, I speak, essentially, for a community which was wiped out.
